section.para: Obedience brings peace.

Marnette woke in the gray light of artificial dawn with the documents still clutched against her chest. She had fallen asleep sitting up, her back against the wall, the gray folder pressed to her body like armor or perhaps like a wound she was trying to hold closed. Her neck ached from the awkward angle, her hands were stiff from gripping the folder all night, and for one disorienting moment she didn't remember where she was or why.
Then memory crashed back over her, and she wished it hadn't.

The documents. The Archive. Project Mayfly. Optimized 50-year lifecycle parameters. Units. Workforce acceptance. Elon. Elom. The signatures at the bottom of the proposal, including Timon Frost, the man who had tried to confess before death claimed him. The cold language about genetic modification protocols and religious framework establishment and workforce management optimization.

She looked at the carving on her bedside table, still turned to face the wall where she had placed it the night before. The benevolent face she had prayed to every day of her life, the divine creator who had supposedly designed her for sacred purpose, the god whose name she had spoken in gratitude and supplication—all of it a lie. All of it designed by the same people who had designed her death. All of it maintained for seven hundred years while generations of her people lived and died according to the parameters set by engineers who had never considered them fully human.

Marnette rose slowly, her body protesting the movement, and crossed to the small basin to wash her face. The water was cool against her skin, grounding her in the physical reality of the moment. She was still here. She was still alive. She still had perhaps twenty-five or thirty years before her cells began their programmed shutdown—before the apoptosis triggers activated and her body systematically destroyed itself according to the specifications written into her DNA before she was born. That was time. That was something.

But time for what? Time to serve in blissful ignorance, as her grandmother had? Time to pray to a false god and thank him for her chains? Time to watch others die without knowing why, to comfort them with lies, to help them accept what should never be accepted?

She dried her face and looked at herself in the small mirror. The same face that had looked back at her for twenty-six years—dark eyes, strong features, the face of a faithful servant of Elom. No, she corrected herself. Not a faithful servant. A genetically engineered workforce unit, as the documents had called her. A resource to be managed, an asset to be optimized, a biological machine designed to serve for fifty years and then terminate so that she would never accumulate enough knowledge or power to threaten the system that had created her.

That's what she was. That's what she had always been, even when she didn't know it. Even when she prayed and served and found meaning in her designated role, she had been nothing more than a product operating within designed parameters.
The morning prayer rose automatically to her lips, the words she had spoken every day since childhood: "Elom, creator and guide, who designed us for purpose..."

She stopped. The words stuck in her throat like broken glass, their meaning transformed from comfort to accusation. Who designed us for purpose. They had designed her, not for divine purpose but for labor efficiency. Not to serve Elom's plan but to serve Masters' convenience. The purpose she had found in her work, in her faith, in her life—all of it had been placed there by engineers who needed her to accept her fate without question.

Marnette sank to her knees beside the bed, not in prayer but in collapse. The grief hit her like a physical blow, doubling her over, forcing the air from her lungs in a sound that was half sob and half scream. Everything she had believed, everything that had given her life meaning and structure and hope—all of it was gone. Not just wrong, but deliberately, maliciously false. A cage built of words and faith, designed to keep her docile until her time ran out.

She had prayed to the man who had enslaved her people. She had thanked him for her chains. She had accepted her shortened life as a gift when it was actually a sentence—a death sentence coded into her very cells, executed slowly over decades rather than quickly in a single blow.

The tears came then, hot and unstoppable, the grief of a lifetime of deception pouring out of her in waves that shook her entire body. She cried for her grandmother, dead at fifty-three, accepting her end with such faith and grace, such certainty that she was returning to Elom's embrace, never knowing she had been murdered by people who had been dead for centuries. She cried for Elder Maris, whose peaceful face in death had once seemed beautiful to Marnette and now seemed tragic—a woman who had spent her final days grateful for her suffering, not knowing it had been designed to end exactly as it did.

She cried for every patient she had ever cared for, every death she had witnessed and accepted as natural when it was anything but. She had held their hands and spoken prayers over their bodies and helped their families find comfort in Elom's design, all while participating in a lie so enormous that she couldn't have seen it even if she had looked. The faith she had offered them wasn't comfort—it was complicity. The acceptance she had helped them find wasn't peace—it was surrender to a system designed to destroy them.

And she cried for herself, for the woman she had been just weeks ago, the faithful servant who had found genuine comfort in prayers and genuine purpose in service, the woman who had believed she was part of something meaningful and good. That woman was gone now, as dead as if her cells had terminated on schedule. What remained was someone else—someone who knew the truth, someone who couldn't unknow it, someone who would have to find a way to live in a world where everything she had believed was a weapon used against her.

When the tears finally subsided, Marnette found herself sitting on the floor with her back against the bed, her face swollen and her throat raw. The folder lay beside her where it had fallen during her collapse, its contents spilled across the worn floor—documents that proved everything she had lost, evidence of a crime that had lasted seven hundred years and claimed billions of victims who never knew they were victims.

She gathered the papers with trembling hands, placing them back in the folder, hiding it once again beneath her sleeping pad. Then she rose, washed her face again, and dressed in her gray tunic with the mechanical precision of someone moving through a nightmare.

She had to go out. She had to face the world that had been revealed to her, had to walk among people who didn't know what she now knew, had to pretend that everything was normal when nothing would ever be normal again.

The corridors of the salve district looked different now. Not physically—the same gray walls, the same flat lighting, the same functional simplicity she had known her entire life. But the meaning had changed. Where she had once seen humble dignity, she now saw deliberate limitation—housing designed to remind salves of their place, lighting designed to be adequate rather than beautiful, everything functional and nothing more. Where she had once seen community, she now saw a population managed like livestock, kept together for efficiency, their movements tracked and their behaviors monitored.
And where she had once seen the evidence of Elom's design, she now saw the architecture of control.

Salves moved past her on their way to work shifts, their gray tunics identical to hers, their faces showing the same weariness she had always accepted as the natural result of meaningful labor. How many of them were in their forties or fifties, their bodies already beginning the slow process of shutdown? How many would be dead in ten years, in five, killed by genetic programming they didn't know they carried? How many would die grateful for their lives, never knowing those lives had been stolen from them?

She wanted to stop them, to grab them by the shoulders and shake them, to shout the truth until they understood. Don't you see? Don't you see what they did to us? We could live longer, love longer, learn longer—but they decided fifty years was enough. They decided we didn't deserve what they kept for themselves.

But what good would that do? They would think she was mad. They would reject the truth as surely as she had rejected it when Frost first spoke it. Belief didn't die easily—she knew that now, knew it from the inside, knew the desperate grip that faith maintained even as evidence tore it apart. She had wanted not to believe even while reading the documents, had searched for alternative explanations even while the proof lay in her hands. Why would anyone else be different?

The communal dining hall was crowded with the morning shift, salves fueling themselves for another day of service. The smell of nutrient paste filled the air—engineered nutrition, Marnette now understood, optimized for workforce health parameters. Not poison, but not nourishment either. Just fuel, calibrated to keep them functional for their designated time.

She found a seat in the corner, far from anyone she knew, and stared at the paste on her plate without eating. The system surrounded her, invisible and inescapable. The food she ate, the clothes she wore, the air she breathed, the faith she had once held—all of it was designed to maintain control. The Masters hadn't just engineered salves' bodies; they had engineered their entire existence, every aspect of life optimized to produce compliant workers who would serve and die without ever threatening the order that sustained their oppressors.

It was brilliant, in its own horrific way. Why maintain control through force when you could maintain it through belief? Why risk rebellion when you could breed acceptance into your workforce's very cells? The salves weren't prisoners—they were products, designed to function within specified parameters, designed to fail when those parameters expired.
"Marnette?"

She looked up to find Lena standing beside her table, a tray of food in her hands and concern on her face. The familiar green stains on her fingers from the hydroponics nutrient solutions, the prayer beads visible beneath her collar, the open expression of a friend who had no idea what Marnette was carrying inside her.
"You look terrible," Lena said, sliding into the seat across from her. "Are you feeling alright? You look like you haven't slept in days."
"I haven't. Not really." The truth was easier than a lie, at least in this small way. "Bad dreams."

"About Master Frost?" Lena's voice softened with sympathy, her hand reaching across the table to cover Marnette's. "It's natural, you know. You were with him at the end, watching him die. Those images stay with you. I remember when grandmother died—I dreamed about her for months afterward."

Images. Yes, there were images staying with Marnette, but not the ones Lena imagined. Not the peaceful face of a dying man or the gentle release of his final breath. But the words on documents signed by that same man centuries ago. The cold language about workforce units and lifecycle parameters. The signatures at the bottom of a proposal that had condemned billions to early death.
"Something like that," Marnette managed.

Lena squeezed her hand. "Give it time. The grief passes, even when it feels like it won't. You'll find your peace again." Her expression shifted to something more tentative, more worried. "Actually, I wanted to talk to you about Thomas."

Thomas. The questioning boy who sensed something wrong without knowing what. The doubter who had asked Marnette about Master Frost, about lifespan variations, about the things that didn't make sense. The one she had told to keep looking, to be careful, not to trust easy answers.
"What about him?"

"He's been worse since you talked to him. More questions, more doubts, more arguments with anyone who tries to help him." Lena's voice dropped to barely above a whisper. "He's started refusing to pray, Marnette. He stands there in church with his mouth closed while everyone else speaks the words. He says he can't say things he doesn't believe, that the prayers feel like lies in his mouth."
Marnette felt her chest tighten. Prayers feeling like lies. She understood that now. She understood it perfectly.

"What did you say to him?" Lena pressed. "He won't tell me what you talked about, but something changed after your conversation. He's more certain now, more determined. Like he's found something to hold onto, but it's not faith."

How could Marnette explain? She had told Thomas to trust his doubt, not knowing she would soon find proof that his doubt was justified. She had encouraged him without giving him the evidence that might have prepared him, might have helped him understand what he was sensing.
"I told him to trust his own mind," she said carefully. "I told him that questions weren't necessarily wrong."

"Questions aren't wrong, but refusing prayer is dangerous." Lena's grip on her hand tightened, her eyes filling with the kind of fear that came from genuine love. "People are starting to notice, Marnette. The priests, the community elders—they're talking about intervention. You know what happens to salves who lose their faith completely."

Marnette knew. Counseling first, gentle pressure to return to the fold. Then supervised prayer, mandatory attendance at services, constant monitoring of words and behavior. Then, for those who persisted in their doubt, isolation protocols—removal from the community, assignment to remote work details, separation from everyone who might be influenced by their dangerous questions.

The Church's mechanisms for handling "outlier individuals," as the documents had called them. Community pressure and, where necessary, isolation protocols. Not punishment exactly—just management. Just handling the products that didn't function according to specifications.
"What do you want me to do?" Marnette asked.

"Talk to him again. Help him find his way back to faith." Lena's eyes were pleading, desperate with sisterly love and fear. "You're good at this—you helped our grandmother accept her end, helped her find peace with Elom's design. You've helped dozens of dying people make their crossing with faith intact. Maybe you can help Thomas too. Maybe you can help him see that his questions don't have to destroy his faith."
Find peace with Elom's design. Help him see that his questions don't have to destroy his faith.

The words hit Marnette like a blow to the chest. Lena was asking her to help Thomas accept the very lies that were crushing her, to guide him back into the cage of false faith, to help him make peace with his own murder. She was asking Marnette to do exactly what the system had designed her to do—to be a mechanism of control, helping the troubled products return to their proper functioning.

She couldn't do it. She couldn't look Thomas in the eyes and tell him to accept what she now knew was a monstrous lie. She couldn't help him find peace with a god who didn't exist, with a design that was actually engineering, with a purpose that was actually exploitation.

But she also couldn't tell Lena the truth—not yet, not here, not like this. The truth was too enormous, too devastating, too dangerous to speak without preparation. If she told Lena now, in this crowded dining hall, with her friend's face full of worry and her hands full of desperate hope—it would shatter her. It would shatter everything, and Marnette wasn't ready to catch the pieces.
"I'll talk to him," she said. "But I can't promise anything. Some doubts don't have easy answers."
Lena nodded, relief washing over her face, her shoulders relaxing slightly. "Thank you. I know you'll find a way to help him. You always find a way to help people."

She rose, squeezed Marnette's hand one more time, and headed off to her shift in the hydroponics section—to tend the plants that had been engineered like everything else, to do her part in a system that had been designed to use her and discard her when her usefulness expired.

Marnette watched her go, watched the gray tunic disappear into the crowd of identical gray tunics, and felt the weight of what she now carried pressing down on her shoulders like the red dust of Mars pressing against the habitat domes.

Lena was her oldest friend. They had grown up together, learned their prayers together, mourned together when Marnette's grandmother died. Lena had held Marnette's hand through every difficult moment of her life, had celebrated every success, had been the constant presence that made the salve district feel like home.

If Marnette told her the truth—really told her, with the documents and the evidence and the full horror of what had been done—would Lena believe her? Would she thank Marnette for opening her eyes, for freeing her from a lifetime of lies? Or would she reject the truth, turn away, report Marnette to the priests as a dangerous influence who had corrupted her brother and now threatened the entire community?
The question had no safe answer. But it demanded an answer nonetheless.

The Church of Elom was empty in the middle of the day, its modest space quiet except for the soft hum of the climate control and the flickering of candles at the altar. Marnette stood in the doorway, looking at the mosaics that depicted the colony's founding: Elon Musk gazing at the red planet, the first habitats rising from Martian soil, the first generation of colonists—both classes, though the mosaics didn't distinguish between them—beginning their new lives on a world that had never been meant for human habitation.

She had once seen these images as sacred history, the visual record of Elom's gift to humanity. Now she recognized them as the first chapter of a carefully constructed lie—propaganda designed to create a mythology that would justify centuries of oppression.

She didn't know why she had come here. Perhaps to test herself, to see if she could stand in this space that had once given her such comfort without falling apart. Perhaps to grieve for what she had lost, to say goodbye to the faith that had shaped her entire life. Perhaps simply because she didn't know where else to go, and the habits of twenty-six years were hard to break even when you knew they had been designed to control you.

The altar waited at the front of the church, simple wood with a carving of Elom above it—the benevolent face, the wise eyes, the expression of divine love and eternal compassion that had watched over every service Marnette had ever attended. She walked slowly down the aisle, her footsteps echoing in the empty space, until she stood before the image she had once revered.

Elom. Elon. The man whose name they had corrupted, whose legacy they had twisted, whose image they had used to justify centuries of oppression. Had he known what would be done in his name? Had he approved of the genetic modifications, the biological castes, the manufactured religion that had been built on the foundation of his memory? Or had his descendants perverted his vision, turned his colony into something he would never have recognized or wanted?

The documents hadn't made that clear. They spoke of "the founder's vision" and "colony protocols" and "implementation phases," but they didn't say whether Elon Musk himself had endorsed the creation of salves. Perhaps it didn't matter. Perhaps he had been a saint whose legacy was stolen, or perhaps he had been a monster whose descendants simply continued his work. Either way, he had been dead for centuries before the system was fully implemented. The guilt belonged to those who had voted for it, maintained it, perpetuated it through generation after generation—including Timon Frost, who had signed his name to the proposal and then spent nearly eight centuries living with the consequences.

Marnette knelt before the altar, not in worship but in something closer to confrontation. The prayer beads were still beneath her collar—she hadn't been able to bring herself to remove them, even now—and her hands found them automatically, the smooth coolness of the stones familiar against her fingers. How many times had she held these beads? How many prayers had she spoken while touching them? How many moments of comfort had they given her, comfort that had been designed to keep her docile?

"Elom," she whispered, and the name felt different on her tongue now. Not divine, but human. Not creator, but creation—the creation of people who had needed a god to justify their crimes. "Elom, creator and guide..."

She tried to continue, tried to speak the words she had said thousands of times, but they wouldn't come. Each phrase she had once spoken with such faith now felt like a confession of her own oppression, an acceptance of the lies that had enslaved her.

Who designed us for purpose. Yes, she had been designed. Engineered. Manufactured like the equipment in the agricultural domes or the filters in the air processing systems. Her purpose wasn't sacred—it was economic. Her role wasn't divine—it was efficient.

Who gave each of us our role in the great colony. Her role was to serve and die, to accept and not question, to find meaning in a life that had been deliberately shortened so that she would never accumulate enough knowledge to threaten those who had created her. The role wasn't a gift—it was a cage.

Help us to serve with devotion, to accept with grace, to find joy in our designated time. Joy in her designated time. Joy in her murder. That's what they were asking—that she be grateful for the knife at her throat, that she thank her killers for the clean cut, that she accept the end of her life as a blessing rather than a crime.

"No," Marnette said aloud, and the word echoed through the empty church like a gunshot, like a declaration of war, like the first sound of a world beginning to change. "No. I will not accept. I will not find joy. I will not thank you for what you've done to us."

She rose from her knees, her body trembling with an anger she had never felt before—not the quick anger of frustration or the hot anger of injustice, but something deeper, colder, more enduring. The anger of someone who had been betrayed so completely that the betrayal had become the foundation of her entire existence. The anger of someone who had finally seen the bars of her cage and understood that they had been invisible by design.

"You lied to us," she said to the carved face of Elom, her voice growing stronger with each word. "For seven hundred years, you lied to us. You created us to serve and die and never know why. You gave us a god to worship so we would thank our oppressors for our oppression. You designed our faith to make us compliant, our prayers to reinforce our chains, our entire understanding of meaning and purpose to serve your convenience."

She reached up and touched the carving, her fingers brushing the smooth wood of the face she had once revered with such devotion. The craftsmanship was beautiful—delicate features, expressive eyes, a mouth that seemed to smile with eternal compassion. Someone had spent hours creating this image, carving holiness into dead wood. Someone had believed, perhaps, that they were creating something sacred.
But it wasn't sacred. It was a tool. A mechanism of control, as precisely engineered as the genetic modifications that had shortened her life.

"My grandmother prayed to you every day of her life," Marnette continued, her voice cracking with grief and fury. "She died believing you had given her fifty-three blessed years, that her time was part of your perfect design. She thanked you with her last breath, Elom. She thanked you for killing her. And you—you were just a man. A dead man whose name they stole and corrupted to keep us enslaved."

Her hand closed into a fist, and for a moment she wanted to strike the carving, to smash it, to destroy this symbol of everything that had been taken from her and everyone she had ever loved. But she stopped herself. Violence against wood wouldn't change anything. The lie was bigger than any carving, older than any building, more pervasive than any single act of destruction could address.
The system couldn't be broken with fists. It had to be broken with truth.

Instead, she turned and walked back down the aisle, leaving the altar behind, leaving the faith that had sustained her for twenty-six years, leaving the woman she had been before she entered the Archive and found the proof of what had been done to her people.
At the door, she paused and looked back one more time at the mosaics, at the images of the founding, at the benevolent carved face watching over the empty church.
"I'm going to tell them," she said, her voice steady now, no longer trembling. "I don't know how, and I don't know who will believe me, but I'm going to tell them. Your lie ends with me."
She stepped out into the corridor, into the gray light of the salve district, into a world that didn't know yet how completely it had been deceived.

Thomas was waiting for her when she returned to her quarters. He sat on the floor outside her door, his maintenance coveralls stained with the day's work, his young face showing the exhaustion of someone fighting a battle he didn't fully understand. His eyes met hers as she approached, and she saw in them the same desperate hunger for answers that had driven her to the Archive.
"Lena said you wanted to talk to me," he said as Marnette approached. "She said you were going to help me find peace with my doubts."

Marnette looked at him—nineteen years old, perhaps forty years left if the variations held, already questioning everything she had questioned, already sensing the wrongness she now understood completely. He was sitting where she had been just weeks ago, on the threshold of a truth that would change everything.
"Come inside," she said, opening her door. "We need to talk. But not about finding peace."
Thomas followed her into the small room, his expression shifting from wariness to something closer to hope. "What do you mean?"

Marnette closed the door behind them, checked that the corridor was empty, and pulled the gray folder from beneath her sleeping pad. Her hands trembled slightly as she held it—this moment, this choice, would change both their lives irrevocably. There was no going back from what she was about to do. Once she showed him, once he knew, they would both be carrying a burden that could never be set down.

"You've been asking questions," she said. "About lifespans, about the Church, about why things are the way they are. You've been told your questions are dangerous, that doubt is a sickness, that you need to find your way back to faith."

Thomas nodded slowly. "Everyone keeps saying that. Lena, the priests, the community elders—they all want me to just accept things I can't accept. But I can't pray to someone I don't believe in. I can't speak words that feel like lies in my mouth."

"Your questions aren't wrong, Thomas. Your doubts aren't sickness." Marnette opened the folder and spread the documents across her small table, the evidence of seven centuries of lies laid out in neat bureaucratic prose. "They're the beginning of the truth."
His eyes went to the papers, then back to her face, confusion and hope warring in his expression. "What is this? Where did you get these?"

"Master Frost's Archive. He spent his final weeks trying to tell me something he couldn't say clearly—something about how things really are, about why we live and die the way we do. After he died, I went back. I found what he wanted me to find." She picked up the proposal, the document with Frost's signature at the bottom, the cold language about workforce units and optimized lifecycle parameters. "I found the truth about everything."

Thomas took the document from her hands, his eyes scanning the words, his face going pale as the meaning sank in. She watched him read, watched the horror dawn in his eyes, watched him travel in minutes a journey that had taken her weeks.
"Fifty-year lifecycle parameters. Workforce units. Genetic modification protocols." He looked up at her, his voice barely above a whisper. "They designed us to die?"

"They designed everything. Our lifespans, our faith, our understanding of ourselves and our place in the world. Elom isn't a god, Thomas. He's a man named Elon Musk, and the people who came after him turned his name into a tool of control." She pointed to another document, the Church establishment protocols. "They created the religion at the same time they created the genetic modifications. The faith we've been taught—it was designed to make us accept our deaths as divine gift instead of engineered murder."
Thomas sank into the only chair, the document still clutched in his hands, his face showing the same devastation Marnette had felt in the Archive. "This can't be real. This can't be true."

"I wish it wasn't. I spent weeks hoping Frost was confused, that the documents were fakes, that there was some other explanation." Marnette knelt beside him, meeting his eyes with the steady gaze of someone who had already traveled this road and emerged on the other side. "But it is true. All of it. We were created to serve and die, and they gave us a god to thank for it."

Thomas was silent for a long moment, staring at the documents as if they might rearrange themselves into something less horrifying. Then he looked up, and instead of the despair Marnette had expected, she saw something else in his eyes—something that looked almost like relief.

"I knew it," he whispered. "I knew something was wrong. I've known for years, but everyone kept telling me I was just doubting, just losing faith, just sick with questions that didn't have answers." He held up the document like evidence in his own defense. "But I wasn't sick. I was right. There was something wrong, and this is it."

"Yes." Marnette felt tears prickling her eyes—not of grief this time, but of recognition. This was what she had needed when she first learned the truth: someone who understood, someone who could share the burden, someone who didn't think she was mad. "You were right. We were all lied to, but you saw through it even without proof."
"What do we do now?" Thomas asked, his voice gaining strength. "We can't just keep this to ourselves. People need to know."

"I know. But telling people is dangerous. Most of them don't want to hear it—they're like I was, so invested in the faith that they'll reject any evidence that threatens it. And if the wrong people find out we have these documents..." She didn't finish the sentence. She didn't need to.

"So we tell the right people," Thomas said, his young face suddenly older, suddenly determined. "We find others like us—people who already doubt, people who might be willing to see. We show them, one at a time, until there are enough of us that they can't silence us all."

Marnette looked at this young man, nineteen years old, transformed in a single conversation from a troubled doubter into someone with purpose and direction. His doubt had made him ready for this moment, had prepared him to receive the truth without being destroyed by it. The system had tried to break him, to force him back into compliance, but it had only made him stronger.
"Who do you trust?" she asked. "Who else might be willing to see?"

Thomas considered the question carefully. "Some of the older maintenance workers—the ones who told me about the pre-Optimization days, the ones who remember when the Church changed some of its teachings. They've been asking questions longer than I have. And there's a woman in the agricultural section, Petra, who stopped going to church last year. She doesn't talk about why, but everyone knows she's lost her faith."
Names. Faces. People who might be ready to hear what Marnette had learned. People who might help carry this burden, spread this knowledge, begin the process of unraveling seven hundred years of lies.

"We'll start with them," Marnette said. "But carefully. We can't rush this, and we can't force anyone to see what they're not ready to see. We show them the evidence, we answer their questions, and we let them make their own choice."
"What about Lena?" Thomas asked. "She's your closest friend. Shouldn't she know?"

Marnette thought of Lena, her green-stained fingers, her sincere prayers, her desperate worry about her brother's lost faith. Lena who believed with her whole heart, who had never questioned, who found genuine comfort in the teachings that Marnette now knew were chains.

"Not yet," she said. "Lena believes. Really believes, in a way that you and I never quite did. The truth might destroy her before it frees her. We need to wait until we're stronger, until we have more people, until we can support her through the breaking."
Thomas nodded slowly. "So we begin."
"We begin," Marnette agreed. "Quietly, carefully, person by person. We tell them the truth, and we let the truth do its work."

She gathered the documents and placed them back in the folder, handling them with the care they deserved—the evidence of humanity's greatest crime, preserved by a dying man who had helped commit it, now passed to two salves who would use it to begin undoing what had been done.
"Tomorrow," she said. "Tomorrow we start building something new."
Thomas rose to leave, but paused at the door. "Marnette? Thank you. For trusting me. For showing me I wasn't crazy."
"You're not crazy," she said. "You're awake. And soon, there will be more of us."

After he left, Marnette sat alone in her small room, the folder in her lap, the weight of what she had begun settling over her like a mantle. She had broken the silence. She had shared the truth with another person. The lie that had lasted seven hundred years had cracked, just slightly, just between two people in a small room in the salve district.
But cracks spread. And lies, once questioned, never quite hold together the same way again.
She looked at the carving of Elom, still turned to face the wall, and felt something that might have been hope stirring in her chest.
The truth was out now. Not far, not yet—but out. And that was a beginning.
